coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic activity of glyphosate over a combination strategy of GQDs/TNAs heterojunction composites
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

Cao, Hao
Lu, Jinren
Li, Yiming
Bao, Mutai
coredata.dc:description: A photocatalytic process was used to effectively remove glyphosate, an emerging pollutant and contaminant, through advanced oxidation. For this purpose, a feasible combination strategy of two-step anodisation and electrodeposition methods were proposed to fabricate graphene quantum dots (GQDs) supported titanium dioxide nanotube arrays (TNAs). The resultant GQDs/TNAs heterojunction composite exhibited significant degradation reactivity and circulation stability for glyphosate due to its excellent photo-generated electron and hole separation ability. After the introduction of GQDs into TNAs, the photodegradation efficiency of glyphosate increased from 69.5% to 94.7% within 60 min under UV–Vis light irradiation (λ = 320–780 nm). By analysing the intermediate products and through the evolvement of heteroatoms during glyphosate photodegradation, alanine and serine were discovered for the first time, and a detailed degradation mechanism of glyphosate was proposed. This study indicates that GQDs/TNAs heterojunction composite can almost completely degrade the glyphosate into inorganics under the appropriate conditions.

Length of error bars represents ± 1 standard deviation (n = 3).

The concentration of HCHO during the degradation process, [Glyphoate] = 5, 10 mg L−1. Length of error bars represents ± 1 standard deviation (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic activity of glyphosate over a combination strategy of GQDs/TNAs heterojunction composites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A photocatalytic process was used to effectively remove glyphosate, an emerging pollutant and contaminant, through advanced oxidation. For this purpose, a feasible combination strategy of two-step anodisation and electrodeposition methods were proposed to fabricate graphene quantum dots (GQDs) supported titanium dioxide nanotube arrays (TNAs). The resultant GQDs/TNAs heterojunction composite exhibited significant degradation reactivity and circulation stability for glyphosate due to its excellent photo-generated electron and hole separation ability. After the introduction of GQDs into TNAs, the photodegradation efficiency of glyphosate increased from 69.5% to 94.7% within 60 min under UV–Vis light irradiation (λ = 320–780 nm). By analysing the intermediate products and through the evolvement of heteroatoms during glyphosate photodegradation, alanine and serine were discovered for the first time, and a detailed degradation mechanism of glyphosate was proposed. This study indicates that GQDs/TNAs heterojunction composite can almost completely degrade the glyphosate into inorganics under the appropriate conditions.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution, one of the most prominent environmental issues of the world, has grabbed significant attention of the entire human society. The major concerns related to this issue include pollution caused by pesticides, herbicides, heavy metals, and other hazardous organic substances. Glyphosate [IUPAC name N-(phosphonomethyl)-glycine)], a synthetic organic substance, has played an important role in agricultural development over the past decades. Owing to its broad-spectrum and efficient herbicidal performance, glyphosate-based herbicides have been developed and widely applied for crop production [1]. The toxicity of glyphosate remains a controversial issue. Glyphosate was once considered as a low toxic and environment-friendly herbicide considering that its herbicidal mechanism can inhibit the shikimate pathway of the plant metabolic process, which in turn interrupts the synthesis of several amino acids that are essential for the survival of plants. While the shikimate pathway is not involved in the metabolic process of animals, glyphosate is generally considered to have low toxicity to animals [2]. However, due to the large scale and intensive use of glyphosate and its accumulation in the environment and agricultural products, concerns related to the safety of using glyphosate-based herbicides have been raised by several researchers and organizations in recent years. Some independent studies have indicated that glyphosate may not be as safe as was assumed previously, which is inconsistent with other research reports [3,4].

It is highly probable that glyphosate-based herbicides can leach from farmland soils into surface waters and ground waters with rainfalls [5]. If these contaminated waters are used as domestic water sources, the herbicides may also enter the drinking water systems. Although the safety of glyphosate remains controversial, removal of its residues from water through some techniques is essential until a reliable conclusion is reached.

Presently, the purification methods based on physics [6,7], biology [8], and chemistry [9,10] have been developed for treating water bodies contaminated with glyphosate-based herbicides. Glyphosate and other pollutants can be effectively separated from water by some physical methods, such as adsorption and membrane separation processes, but the molecular structure is not completely broken, and the collected residues warrant further treatment by other methods. The biological methods are usually deemed suitable for the treatment of glyphosate production wastewater, which contains a high concentration of glyphosate and other pollutants. However, these methods may not demonstrate satisfactory efficiency with respect to the treatment of low-concentration glyphosate in water, even after several treatment cycles. In addition, studies have demonstrated that the biodegradation process of glyphosate may result in production of stronger toxic and stable (aminomethyl)phosphonic acid (AMPA) [11]. The chemical methods available to degrade glyphosate in water mainly include oxidation methods, such as the conventional oxidation processes and advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) [12,13]. Photocatalytic technology, an important type of AOPs, can help overcome the limitations of low efficiency and incomplete degradation associated with other methods as well as demonstrate good potential in the degradation of low-concentration organic matters.

Since the discovery of TiO2 electrodes for water splitting in 1972 [14], the interest in the development of semiconductor-based photocatalysts has grown exponentially in the science and engineering community. Among the numerous photocatalytic materials available, TiO2 has received widespread attention because of its stability, nontoxicity, economic factor, and highly reactive nature [15]. Therefore, TiO2 is widely used in systems of air purification, water purification [16,17], and new energy development [18]. In the process of research and application of TiO2, a novel structure of self-organised oxide nanotube arrays has grabbed increasing attention because it presents a large surface area and a short diffusion distance of photogenerated electron/hole [19]. The synthesis methods of TiO2 nanotubes mainly include the sol − gel method [20], hydrothermal synthesis [21], and anodization [22]. When compared with the other synthesis methods, TiO2 nanotubes synthesised using the anodic oxidation method are dense, orderly, and unified. Although the structure of the TiO2 nanotube array is unique and fascinating, the inherent characteristics of large band gaps (Eg = 3.2 eV) and rapid photogenerated carrier recombination have restricted its further application [23]. To inhibit the recombination of electrons and holes, several research strategies have been developed, such as the introduction of heteroatoms [24], coupling with other semiconductors [25], and deposition of noble metals [26].

Graphene (GR) is a two-dimensional carbon material with excellent mechanical, electrical, and thermal potential, as well as high electrical conductivity, mobility, large surface area, and strong adsorbing capacity for organic molecules [27]. GR quantum dots (GQDs) are quasi-zero-dimensional materials derived from GR, which exhibits excellent solubility, photoelectric property, chemical stability, fine biocompatibility, and low toxicity. These characters endow GQDs with significant potential for applications, such as bio-imaging [28], production of biomarkers [29], light-emitting diodes [30], and solar cells [31], and photocatalysis [32]. As a powerful component of energy conversion, GQDs are an ideal photosensitiser for coupling semiconductors. Past researchers have used GQDs as photosensitisers to enhance the photoelectric property of TiO2 and other semiconductor materials, such as SnO2, CdS, ZnO, Fe2O3, and g-C3N4 [33]. Min et al. [34] reported strongly coupled TiO2/GQDs composites through a facile one-step hydrothermal method and evaluated the photocatalytic H2 evolving activity of the composites. Gupta et al. [35] proposed an impregnation method for the fabrication of GQDs infilled TiO2 NTAs hybrid structure for the degradation of MB. Azimirad et al. [36] constructed the GQDs/hierarchical porous TiO2 nanocomposites by using spin coating and electrophoretic methods. Moreover, a detailed understanding of the photo enhancement was gained based on a charge-transfer mechanism. Pan et al. [37] reported a molecular fusion method for the fabrication of amine-functionalised GQDs and prepared GQD/TiO2 heterojunctions for the degradation of MO. Additionally, a mixed strategy of GQDs and TiO2 nanotubes was developed for degrading several organic pollutants. To the best of our knowledge, only a few studies have investigated the heterojunction structure of GQDs and TiO2 nanotube arrays for the degradation of glyphosate.

Herein, the high-density and well-ordered GQDs/TNAs composites were acquired on flat titanium substrates through a simple combination of two-step anodization and electrodeposition methods. A series of characterisations, including scanning electron microscope (SEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL), and electron spin resonance (ESR), was employed to research the structural, optical, and electrical properties of the samples. Moreover, the photocatalytic activity was evaluated through photocatalytic degradation of glyphosate under UV–Vis light irradiation. Compared with the pure TNAs material, the photocatalytic reactivity of the GQDs/TNAs composites was enhanced, indicating a significant potential for environmental remediation. Most importantly, the detailed degradation mechanism of glyphosate was elucidated after analysing the degradation intermediates. Therefore, the present study may provide a valuable reference for the research on glyphosate degradation.

Ti plates (purity 99.9%) were purchased from Baoji Zhijie Titanium Industry Co., Ltd, China. GQDs of an average lateral size 5–15 nm were provided by the Qingdao Haida Haixi New Materials Co., Ltd, China. Ethylene glycol [(CH2OH)2, purity 99.5%], tertiary butanol (TBA, purity 99.5%), l-Ascorbic acid (VC, purity 99.7%), 2,3-Dichloro-5,6-dicyano-1,4-benzoquinone (DDQ, purity 98%), disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA-2Na, purity 99%) and ammonium fluoride (NH4F, purity 96%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Glyphosate (purity 97.9%), AMPA (purity 98.0%), glycolic acid (purity 98.0%), glycine (purity 99.0%), l-alanine (purity 99.9%), and l-serine (purity 99.9%) were purchased from A ChemTek Inc. Sarcosine (purity 99.0%) and N,O-bis(trimethylsilyl)trifluoroacetamide with 1% trimethylchlorosilane (BSTFA with 1% TMCS) were bought from the Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Dichloromethane (purity 99.9%) was supplied by ACS. Deionised water and ultrapure water were used for all the experiments.

The TNAs photocatalysts were prepared through a two-step anodization process. Before anodization, the Ti plates were mechanically polished. The Ti plates and Pt electrodes, which were being used as anodes and cathodes, respectively, were anodised in an ethylene glycol solution (with the constant potential of 60 V) containing 0.5 wt% NH4F and 2 vol% H2O [38]. The exposed area of the electrodes to the electrolyte was 10 × 20 mm in effect. Ti plate with the oxide layer was processed with ultrasound in deionised water, until the nanotubes completely fell off, 2 h after the first anodization. The second 1-h anodization was conducted in the same manner after the materials were entirely dry at room temperature. The prepared samples were rinsed with deionised water and dried at room temperature, followed by annealing in a muffle furnace at 450 °C for 2 h to obtain the TNAs photocatalysts.

GQDs/TNAs heterojunction composites were prepared by introducing GQDs to the TNAs surface through electrodeposition. The Pt electrode and the prepared TNAs photocatalyst were used as the anode and cathode respectively. Under a 2 V direct current voltage, the GQDs attached well to the inner walls and the tube cap of the prepared TNAs. The electrodeposition of GQDs occurred in 6.5 min, and its concentration was 0.05 mg mL−1. Finally, the prepared samples were rinsed with sufficient deionised water, followed by obtaining GQDs/TNAs heterojunction composites after drying.

The observation of the surface morphology and the structure of the samples was performed using a field emission SEM (S-4800, Hitachi, Japan). The internal structure of the samples was observed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2010, JEOL, Japan). The elemental compositions of the optimum sample were analysed using field emission SEM (QUANTA FEG250, FEI, USA) equipped with EDS. The crystalline structures of the samples were identified by XRD (D8-Advance, Bruker, Germany) under the Cu Kα radiation. The surface properties and composition of the samples were analysed through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The contents of GQDs in the samples were determined by thermogravimetric analysis (TG, Diomond TG, Perkin Elmer, USA) and heated to 800 ℃ at the rate of 10 ℃ min−1 in a nitrogen ambient atmosphere. The optical absorption properties of the solid samples were measured with the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–Vis DRS, UV-250, Shimadzu, Japan). The photoluminescence properties of the samples were identified through photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL, FluoroMax 4, HORIBA, USA) with the excitation wavelength of 280 nm and the emission wavelength of 400 nm. The vibration and rotational levels of the samples were measured by Raman spectroscopy (DXR 2Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The electrical properties of the samples were analysed by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS, Reference 600, Gamry, USA) and photovoltage response (Reference 600, Gamry). The free radicals produced by photocatalyst were analysed using an electron spin resonance spectrometer (ESR, JES-FA200, JEOL).

Photocatalytic degradation of glyphosate was conducted in a quartz beaker on a magnetic stirrer containing 35 mL of glyphosate solution of specific concentrations (typically 5 mg L−1 and 10 mg L−1). The prepared GQDs/TNAs photocatalyst, which was composed of three photocatalytic electrodes arranged in parallel, was placed vertically in the reactor, and a xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300, 320–780 nm) was used to simulate light. The residue of glyphosate in the water was measured at regular intervals, and the degradation efficiency was calculated according to the following formula:
(1)
R
( % )
=
C 0
-
C t
C 0
× 100 %
where R represents the degradation efficiency of glyphosate, C0 is the initial concentration of glyphosate at the equilibrium point of desorption, and Ct is the concentration of glyphosate at time t during irradiation.

In this study, the concentrations of glyphosate, phosphate (PO4 3−), nitrate (NO3 −), ammonium (NH4 +), and formaldehyde were measured according to the standard methods with an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV-2540, Shimadzu) [39,40]. Total organic carbon (TOC) was determined by the TOC analyser (multi-N/C3100, JENA, German). The degradation intermediates of glyphosate were qualitatively identified by gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) (TRACE1310-TSQ8000Evo, Thermo Scientific, USA) based on the methods of Guérette et al. [41] and de Paiva et al. [42], with slight modifications. Briefly, aproximately 200 mL of water sample with a degradation time of 30 min was dried in a vacuum freeze dryer at − 80 ℃ for 36 h, and the residue was collected and dispersed into dichloromethane solvent, and then transferred to a graduated glass tube. The mixture was evaporated to 1.5 mL at room temperature under N2 and then transferred to a glass GC vial. Next, 200 μL of BSTFA with 1% TMCS was added for derivatization at 60 ℃ for 2 h. Finally, the derivatized sample was injected for GC–MS analysis. To further identify the intermediate products produced in the degradation process, several standard compounds including glyphosate, AMPA, glycolic acid, glycine, l-alanine, l-serine and sarcosine were derivatized under the same reaction conditions, and analyzed by GC − MS. The chromatographic separation was performed with a TG-5MS-fused-silica capillary column of 30-m length, 0.25-mm internal diameter, and 0.25-µm film thickness. The oven temperature programme was set as follows: held at 50 °C for 4 min, ramped to 150 °C at 6 °C min−1 and then held for 4 min, finally ramped to 270 °C at 6 °C min−1 and held for 10 min. Helium (99.99%) was used as the carrier gas at a flow rate of 1.2 mL min−1. The mass spectrometer was operated in an electron impact (EI) mode at 70 eV. The ion source temperature was 230 °C, and the transfer line temperature was maintained at 280 °C. The analysis was performed in the full scan mode (range 30–550 m/z). In addition, the amino acids produced in the degradation process were quantitatively measured according to the method described by Zhang et al [43].

The SEM characterization demonstrated that the nozzle of the TNAs (Fig. 1 a) was clean before loading. As illustrated in Fig. 1a, pure TNAs photocatalyst exhibited a uniform and dense tubular structure, with a moderate tube length of approximately 10 μm (Fig. 1c) and an inner diameter of approximately 110 nm (Fig. 1a), which can appropriately accommodate the GQDs. To investigate the effect of GQDs loading on the microstructure of GQDs/TNAs, the deposition time was controlled at 6.5 min. The samples obtained correspondingly were denoted as GQDs/TNAs-6.5. Fig. 1b demonstrates the GQDs/TNAs heterojunction composite, with an inner diameter of approximately 100 nm, indicating that the loading of GQDs can narrow the nanotube diameter. To observe the distribution of GQDs deposited on TNAs more intuitively, TEM was used to observe the samples of TNAs and GQDs/TNAs-6.5. Fig. 1d depicts the TEM image of the nanotube of pure TNAs. As shown in the Fig. 1d, the internal wall of pure TNAs was clean and smooth. Moreover, the TEM image (Fig. 1e) revealed that GQDs gathered inside the nanotube of the TNAs. The HRTEM image of GQDs/TNAs-6.5 in Fig. 1f shows GR single crystals, with a spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to that of GR (100) planes [44], and an interplanar spacing of 0.35 nm, corresponding to the (101) planes of anatase TiO2, further indicating that GQDs were successfully surrounded on the surface of TiO2. The elemental composition and distribution of GQDs/TNAs composites were analyzed with the EDS spectrometer (Fig. 1g-l). The peaks of Ti, O, and C elements were observed on the corresponding SEM − EDS mapping image in Fig. 1e-h. The elements Ti and O were mainly derived from TNAs, and element C was mostly derived from GQDs. Furthermore, the number of Ti and O elements (Fig. 1l) was found to be the most abundant, which circumstantially indicated that the main component of the composite was TiO2. It is thus evident that the GQDs are uniformly supported on the TiO2 nanotubes in the distribution map of element C (Fig. 1k).

The chemical composition of the GQDs/TNAs photocatalyst was obtained through XPS measurements. Fig. 2 a shows the XPS survey spectrum of the GQDs/TNAs photocatalyst, indicating that the sample was mainly composed of C, O, and Ti elements. The O 1 s spectrum in Fig. 2b presents two peaks at 529.7 and 532 eV, which correspond to the Ti-O bond in the TNAs and the OH bond in the GQDs, respectively [45]. The 1 s core-level XPS spectra of C clearly indicate that the peaks appeared at 284.9 and 288.4 eV, which respectively corresponded to the CC bond and O-CO bond in the GQDs [46]. The Ti 2p core-level XPS spectrum is shown in Fig. 2d. The peaks at 458.6 and 464.2 eV in this figure can be assigned to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively, and this result is in good agreement with the binding energy values of the Ti4+ oxidation state [13].

The crystalline structures of Ti, TNAs, and GQDs/TNAs photocatalysts were determined by XRD (Fig. 3 a). As illustrated in Fig. 3a, the diffraction peaks at 2θ of 25.3°, 37.8°, 48.1°, 53.9°, 55.1°, 62.7°, 68.9°, 70.4°, and 75.1° corresponded to the crystallographic structures of (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (116), (220), and (215) of anatase TiO2, with no characteristic diffraction peak of the rutile phase, indicating that the crystal form of TiO2 was anatase, which was consistent with the HRTEM analysis results. Moreover, GQDs doping did not change the crystal form of TNAs, indicating that GQDs cannot enter the crystal structure of the TNAs and cannot affect its crystal form. No characteristic peak of GR was detected at approximately 24.5°, possibly because of the low GQDs content or being covered by a characteristic peak of anatase TiO2 at 25.3° [47].

The GQDs that were doped through electrochemical methods were fewer on the surface of the TNAs. Hence, the peak intensity of the Raman spectra was weaker than that of the TNAs. Therefore, the Raman spectrum was assigned into two portions for analysis (Fig. 3b and Fig.S1). GQDs/TNAs-2, GQDs/TNAs-5, and GQDs/TNAs-8 match the doping time of 2, 5, and 8 min, respectively. In Fig. 3b, TNAs and GQDs/TNAs at different doping times showed the characteristic peaks of anatase TiO2 at 148 cm−1 (Eg(1)), 396 cm−1 (B1g(1)), 516 cm−1 (B1g(2) + A1g(1)), and 635 cm−1 (Eg(2)), indicating that the crystal form after calcination is anatase at 450 °C without any form of rutile [48], meanwhile indicating that introducing the GQDs does not affect the crystal form of TNAs. The D peak near 2700 cm−1 is a 2D GR peak, and this size represents the thickness of the GQDs (Fig. S1) , suggesting that the intensity of the peak increases gradually with an increase in doping time, possibly because of the increase in doping amount of the GQDs with the electrodeposition time. The G peak of graphite appeared at 1570 cm−1, which is a characteristic peak of crystalline carbon produced by the graphite base surface and a strong tangential mode absorption band [49]. Generally, the intensity of this peak varies in proportion with the graphite lamellae.

Fig. 3c depicts the photoluminescence spectra of the TNAs and the GQDs/TNAs photocatalysts at different deposition times. As presented in Fig. 3c, the signal exhibited the lowest intensity in the spectrum 5 min after the doping started, and this intensity was lower than TNAs, indicating that GQDs can effectively capture the electrons, thereby inhibiting the recombination of the electron-hole and improving the photocatalytic performance. Meanwhile, the peak signal intensified significantly than TNAs at the doping time of 8 min, which indicates that more GQDs can convene in TiO2 nanotubes, the excess GQDs will absorb more incident light with increasing doping time, thus affecting the light transmission, resulting in the weakening of the light energy involved in the photocatalytic process.

Fig. 3d demonstrates the UV–Vis DRS of TNAs and GQDs/TNAs photocatalysts at different deposition times. As presented in Fig. 3d, the GQDs/TNAs photocatalysts at different deposition times absorbed more intensity of light than the TNAs photocatalysts in the visible range of 400–800 nm. In the 300–400-nm range under the ultraviolet irradiation, the GQDs/TNAs-5 photocatalyst showed a higher optical absorption than the TNAs photocatalysts, which indicates that the introduction of GQDs under certain conditions can increase the optical absorption efficiency of TNAs under both visible and ultraviolet light sources. Meanwhile, the photocatalysts of GQDs/TNAs-2 and GQDs/TNAs-8 showed a lower absorption efficiency under the ultraviolet light than the TNAs photocatalysts, possibly because of the integrated effect of photoabsorption and photoelectron conduction [50]. At the deposition time of 2 min, the introduced amount of GQDs was little. As a result, only the activated electrons showed a short conduction time and could easily combine with the holes for GQDs with excellent photoelectron conductivity. At the deposition time of 8 min, the introduced GQD amount was excessive, which reduced the light transmittance. This phenomenon revealed an appropriate range of the deposited GQDs amount, which being excessive or very little can affect the performances of the composites.

Fig. 4 a depicts the photovoltage responses of TNAs and GQDs/TNAs photocatalysts. In Fig. 4a, TNAs and GQDs/TNAs both showed rapid responses to each on–off photoperiod. The open-circuit voltages of the TNAs and GQDs/TNAs photocatalysts were maintained at − 62 mV and − 85 mV, respectively before the light was turned on. Under the light source, the photovoltages of the TNAs and the GQDs/TNAs photocatalysts decreased rapidly by approximately 90 and 109 mV each. When compared with TNAs, GQD's introduction led to a lower initial potential of GQDs/TNAs. The illumination activates the generation of photoelectrons and the electron transition and accumulation on the TNAs conduction band [51], which resulted in a rapid increase in the photovoltage of TNAs and GQDs/TNAs. Considering the superior performance of GQDs in trapping and conducting charge carriers, the optical response of the GQDs/TNAs was stronger than that of the TNAs. The electrons produced by TNAs could be effectively captured in GQDs/TNAs systems through the two-dimensional π-π conjugated structure of GQDs, with GQDs acting as the transporters to rapidly propel the charge to the external circuits [52]. The GQDs were introduced to facilitate the improvement of the efficiency of separating the charge and inhibiting the recombination of photoelectron-hole pairs, thereby enhancing the photoelectric response of GQDs/TNAs.

In addition, the charge-transfer inhibition process has been reported to be caused by the double-layer capacitance and charge-transfer resistance, which can be evaluated through the high-frequency arc diameter in the EIS Nynquist plot. A smaller diameter indicates a decrease in electrical resistance on the solid interface and the solid–liquid junction, which can accelerate the electron transfer process [53]. As shown in Fig. 4b, the interfacial features of the TNAs and GQDs/TNAs photocatalysts were determined by EIS measurements under dark and light environments. Fig. 4b shows that the GQDs/TNAs photocatalysts possessed smaller arc diameters as the TNAs photocatalysts under both dark and light environments. Together, the results indicated that the introduction of GQDs increased the ability of the TNAs photocatalyst to transfer the electrons and improved the capability of photocatalysis.

Fig. 5 presents the equivalent circuits of TNAs and GQDs/TNAs photocatalysts under dark and light conditions. As shown in Fig. 5, Rs includes the solution resistance and the uncompensated potential drop. Rn and Q1 are the resistance and the capacitance of the solid interface layer, respectively, resulting from the passivation reaction between the electrode surface and the electrolyte. Rct and Q2 are the charge-transfer resistance and the double-layer capacitance on the photo reflector, respectively. W is the Warburg impedance caused by the mass transfer of electrolyte to the reaction sit [54]. To further explain the electron transfer ability of GQDs/TNAs composites, the instantaneous photocurrent of TNAs and GQDs/TNAs was explored. As shown in Fig. S2, the GQDs/TNAs composite could produce a higher current value than TNAs under light conditions, which is consistent with the photovoltage test results.

The contents of GQDs in GQDs/TNAs composites were determined by TG analysis. As shown in Fig. S3, the TGA curve of GQDs/TNAs exhibited a slight downward slope below 190 ℃, which was attributed to the desorption of water formed by the condensation reaction of terminal hydroxyl group of GQDs [55]. Moreover, the weight loss of GQDs/TNAs composites above 190 °C could be attributed to thermal decomposition of GQDs. Theoretically, GQDs have been completely decomposed when the temperature reached 600 ℃. The mass loss of GQDs/TNAs composites at a temperature of 600–800 ℃ might be attributed to the slow overflow of GQD decomposition products from TiO2 nanotubes. According to the mass loss of the samples in the temperature range of 190–800 °C, the estimated GQDs contents of GQDs/TNAs-2, GQDs/TNAs-5, and GQDs/TNAs-8 were 1.5%, 3.1%, and 4.7%, respectively.

To determine the contribution of free radicals to the degradation of glyphosate in the GQDs/TNA system, the radical quenching experiments were performed using TBA, VC, DDQ, and EDTA-2Na as ∙OH, ∙O2 −, e−, and h+ quenchers, respectively [56]. Fig. 6 a shows that the degradation rate of glyphosate was reduced to 34% and 61% by adding TBA and VC in the GQDs/TNAs systems, respectively. However, with the addition of DDQ and EDTA-2Na, the degradation rate of glyphosate in the GQDs/TNAs system could still reach 95% and 91%, respectively, indicating that ∙OH and ∙O2 − play a dominant role in the degradation of glyphosate. To further verify the generation of radicals in the GQDs/TNAs system, the ESR spectra of the TNAs and the GQDs/TNAs photocatalysts were explored. As shown in Fig. 6b and Fig. 6c, the amount of ∙O2 − and ∙OH generated from the GQDs/TNAs photocatalyst was significantly higher than that of the TNAs photocatalyst under the same environment. This result further confirms that the introduction of GQDs can accelerate the conduction of photogenerated electrons, inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thereby improving the photocatalytic reactivity of the composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of glyphosate by GQDs/TNAs photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The depiction in Fig. 7 d suggests that the GQDs/TNAs photocatalyst has stronger photocatalytic activity. In terms of glyphosate degradation, after 60 min of UV–Vis light irradiation, glyphosate gained a 94.7% degradation rate. It is evident from Fig. 7d that the kinetic constants of glyphosate degradation of the GQDs/TNAs were 4.89 × 10−2, which is 2.5-times greater than that of TNAs (1.96 × 10−2). In addition, the degree of mineralization of glyphosate in the GQDs/TNAs system was explored. As displayed in Fig. 7e, the TOC removal efficiency reached 95% in 120 min when the initial concentration of glyphosate was 5 mg L−1. When the initial concentration of glyphosate increased to 10 mg L−1, the TOC removal efficiency was only 55%, which can be mainly attributed to the total amount of free radicals produced by the GQDs/TNAs system that is insufficient to completely mineralize the intermediate product of glyphosate.

A large number of ions in the natural water may affect the photocatalytic degradation of the contaminants present in the water. In this paper, Cl–, SO4 2–, NO3 –, Fe3+, and Mn2+ were selected as the objects, according to the quality of surface water, groundwater, and drinking water in China, and different ion concentrations were set to determine the effects of these ions on the glyphosate photodegradation. The effect exerted by Cl–, SO4 2–, and NO3 –on the degradation is depicted in Fig. 7a-c. As can be seen from Fig. 7a, Cl– has an inhibitory function in the degradation process, which gains momentum with increasing Cl− concentration and tends to stabilize after reaching a certain degree. SO4 2– also showed the same inhibitory function to the degradation process (Fig. 7b). The inhibitory effect of SO4 2– was stronger than that of Cl– at low concentrations, and it lost momentum with increasing concentrations. Unlike SO4 2– and Cl–, NO3 – could improve the degradation efficiency of glyphosate (Fig. 7c), which in turn promotes the function in proportion with the NO3 – concentration. Cl– and SO4 2– exhibited inhibitory effects, possibly due to the strong adsorption possessed by Cl– and SO4 2–, which in turn allows the ions to adsorb on the surface of the photocatalyst, lowers the adsorption point of glyphosate, and reduces the concentration of glyphosate on the surface of the photocatalysts [57]. The weakening of glyphosate adsorption affects the degradation efficiency of glyphosate to a certain extent. Cl– is known as a hole scavenger that can capture the holes on the surface of the photocatalyst and hence reduce the degradation efficiency. Furthermore, Cl– unites with highly active free radicals, such as ∙OH, and then blocks the reaction between ∙OH and glyphosate [58]. Moreover, chlorine ion reacts with chlorine radicals to form the dichloro radical (Cl2
∙− , Eθ = 2 V), and excessive dichloro radical can scavenge ∙OH that blocks the reaction between ∙OH and glyphosate [59]. The specific reaction processes that ensue are as follows:
(2) OX∙ + Cl– → Cl
(3) h+ + Cl– → Cl∙
(4)
∙OH + Cl∙ → HOCl
(5) Cl∙ + Cl∙ → Cl2
(6) Cl– + Cl∙ → Cl2
∙−
(7) Cl2
∙− + ∙OH → HClO + Cl–

However, the inhibitory effect of SO4 2– decreases with increasing concentrations, which may be attributed to the formation of sulfate radical (SO4 – ∙) through the recombination of SO4 2− and holes. SO4 – ∙ possesses a strong oxidation capability that can counteract the adsorption-induced inhibition to a certain extent. The SO4 – · formation process is as follows[60]:
(8) h+ + SO4 2− → SO4 – ∙

Research has shown that ∙OH can be produced through NO3 – under UV light irradiation, which is conducive to the degradation of glyphosate. Meanwhile, NO3 – can accelerate the transfer of electrons and promote the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. The specific reaction mechanism that ensues is as follows [61]:
(9) NO3 – + hν → NO3 – ∙ → NO2 – ∙ + O– ∙
(10) O– ∙ + H2O → ∙OH + OH–

As shown in Fig. 8 , both Fe3+ and Mn2+ boosted glyphosate degradation. The only difference lay in the improvement of the degradation efficiency of Fe3+, which grew proportionately with the concentration of glyphosate, while it was opposite for Mn2+. The positive effect exerted by Fe3+ on the degradation of glyphosate can be attributed to the following: (1) on addition into the system, Fe3+ can produce H+, which is conducive to the improvement of the degradation efficiency, (2) Fe3+ adsorbs onto the surface of GQDs/TNAs photocatalysts and reacts with electrons, thereby effectively inhibiting the recombination of electrons and holes and enhancing the degradation efficiency, and the specific enhancement mechanism that ensues is as follows:
(11) Fe3+ + e– → Fe2+

The reason why Mn2+ could improve the degradation efficiency maybe because Mn2+ was reduced to Mn in the experiments, and Mn can narrow the bandgap of TiO2 [62]. However Mn2+ could reduce the light transmittance of the solution with increasing concentration, thereby decreasing the resultant degradation efficiency.

The effects of the initial concentration on the glyphosate degradation were analysed at the concentrations of 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 mg L−1 (Fig. 9 a-b). As shown in Fig. 9a, the degradation efficiency of glyphosate increased with time, until it reached 100%. For the same duration, the degradation efficiency of glyphosate decreased with an increase in the initial concentration. The glyphosate degradation curves were fitted using the Langmuir–Hinshelwood equation, and the results obtained conformed to the first-order kinetic equation, as follows (Fig. 9b):
(12)
- ln (
C t
C 0
) = k t
where C0 represents the initial concentration of glyphosate, Ct is the concentration of glyphosate at the degradation time t, and k is the first-order rate constant of the degradation process. The calculated first-order rate constants were 8.21 × 10−2, 5.36 × 10−2, 4.97 × 10−2, 3.66 × 10−3, and 4.02 × 10−2, in accordance with the initial glyphosate concentrations of 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 mg L−1 respectively.

The first-order rate constant indicated the fundamental trend of gradually decrease with increasing initial concentration, as the activate groups (∙OH and ∙O2 –) were the major glyphosate degrading substances during the photocatalytic degradation. Under the same experimental conditions, the amount of the produced activate groups ∙OH and ∙O2 – was stable, and the amount of glyphosate degradation per unit of time appeared to be constant as well. Hence, the first-order rate constant decreased with increasing initial glyphosate concentration. In the meantime, intermediates from the degradation process should compete with glyphosate for the adsorption sites and the active groups [63]. In addition, the light transmittance of the solution decreased with increasing initial concentration because the glyphosate has low solubility, which also leads to a decrease in the received light intensity and the light utilisation efficiency of the GQDs/TNAs photocatalyst [64]. As shown in Fig. 9b, when the initial concentration of glyphosate increased from 8 mg L-1 to 10 mg L-1, the first-order rate constant increased abnormally. This phenomenon can attributed to the glyphosate is more competitive in competing for the adsorption sites and activate groups than the intermediates when the initial concentration is dense, which in turn leads to preferential degradation of glyphosate and an increase in the first-order rate constant.

Being an important parameter in the photocatalytic process, the initial pH was evaluated to determine the effects of pH on the degradation of glyphosate. As shown in Fig. 9c, the glyphosate degradation efficiency decreased with increasing pH. The first-order kinetic equation of glyphosate degradation was accordingly fitted using the Langmuir–Hinshelwood equation (Fig. 9d), and the corresponding rate constants were 1.82 × 10−1, 6.60 × 10−2, 4.41 × 10−2, 4.34 × 10−2, and 3.32 × 10−2 at the initial pH of 2.1, 3.1, 4.7, 6.0, and 7.1, respectively. The main reasons for the first-order rate constant to have an inverse trend with the initial pH are as follows: (1) The H+ concentration was higher in the acidic environment and the positive charge was conducive to the conduction of electrons and prevention of the recombination of electrons and holes, which is propitious for generating the active groups ∙OH and ∙O2 – [65], their amounts were directly linked with the degradation efficiency, (2) Glyphosate is more adsorptive in an acidic environment, which resulted in more glyphosate adsorption on the surface of GQDs/TNAs photocatalysts. This phenomenon could have expedited the glyphosate degradation [66], (3) With an increase in pH, the CO2 produced during the degradation was more likely to release CO3 2– or HCO3 –, which is the ∙OH capturing agent. Consequently, a decrease in the amount of ∙OH reduces the degradation efficiency. HCO3 – possesses good adsorption ability, which may reduce the amount of glyphosate adsorption and challenge the degradation process [54].

In accordance with the analysis results of GC–MS, eight degradation products were identified during the glyphosate degradation process through mass spectrum library search, standard compound comparison, and references [41,42], namely glycolic acid, alanine, glycine, oxalic acid, serine, phosphoric acid, methyl phosphonic acid, and AMPA (Fig. 10 and Table 1. ). Based on the intensity of chromatographic peaks, it is obvious that glycine, phosphoric acid, and AMPA were the main products produced in the degradation process and that their contents were significantly greater than those of other products. Because no signal of acetic acid and sarcosine was detected in the qualitative analysis results, no evidence could confirm whether these substances are produced in the degradation process. Unexpectedly, we found two other intermediates in the degradation process of glyphosate with GQDs/TNAs, alanine, and serine, which have not been reported so far. Quantitative analysis revealed that the content of glycine was much greater than that of alanine and serine (Fig. 11 a). However, the production of the three amino acids was not stable at each time point of the parallel degradation process. Moreover, the content of each amino acid varied significantly in the different batches of experiments. In addition, several unlabelled peaks were noted in the chromatogram, some of which belonged to the ghost peaks produced with excessive derivatization reagents, while others could not be identified accurately. However, whether these peaks are related to the degradation process needs to be further analyzed.

Furthermore, the concentrations of inorganic products produced during the glyphosate degradation process were determined (Fig. 11b). It was noted that the levels of inorganic products were relatively stable. At the end of the degradation test, the content of PO4 3– was 93% of the theoretical value when the concentration of glyphosate was 10 mg L−1. While the content of PO4 3– was in full compliance with the theoretically produced value when the concentration of glyphosate was 5 mg L−1. After the degradation time exceeded 90 min, the increasing trend of NH4 + concentration became relatively flat when the glyphosate concentration is 10 mg L−1. Moreover, NH4 + concentration showed a decreasing trend due to its further oxidation when the glyphosate concentration is 5 mg L-1. The NO3 – production also indicated an increasing trend, albeit with a smaller growth rate, indicating that the oxidation of NH4 + into NO3 – is a slow reaction process even in the GQDs/TNAs system. In Fig. 11b, it can be noted that the sum of ammonium-nitrogen and nitrate-nitrogen concentrations in the low-concentration system was approximately 93% of the theoretical total nitrogen at the end of the degradation, which indicates that there are other products besides NO3 − in the NH4 + oxidation process. Comparison of the production of inorganic products with TOC led to the inference that the CP and CN bonds of the related molecules are more suspectable to breakage than the CC and CH bonds in the degradation process of glyphosate.

Meanwhile, the formaldehyde content produced in the degradation of glyphosate was determined. The results revealed that formaldehyde was a ubiquitous intermediate product (Fig. 12 ), which indirectly proved that formic acid is also produced in the reaction system. As shown in this figure, the content of formaldehyde continuously increased within 120 min when the initial glyphosate concentration was high, while its content first showed an increasing trend and then a decreasing trend at a low glyphosate initial concentration, and these results were roughly consistent with those of TOC. These findings together indicated that the mineralisation rate of glyphosate largely depends on its initial concentration in the same reaction system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Deduction of the glyphosate degradation mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Researchers have reported four possible degradation pathways of glyphosate under different degradation systems. For instance, Barrett et al. [67] speculated that the CP bond of glyphosate could break under the action of manganese oxide to produce sarcosine, which is then converted into glycine. Chen et al. [68] studied the photodegradation of glyphosate under the ferric oxalate system and speculated the possibility of another pathway in the degradation process based on the mechanism proposed by Barrett et al. [67], that is, the CN bond split to form acetic acid and AMPA. In addition, Muneer et al. [69] speculated that there might be two degradation pathways of glyphosate in the TiO2 photocatalysis system, where the CP and CN bonds were broken to produce sarcosine and glycine, respectively. Echavia et al. [70] considered that CP and CN bonds may break during the degradation process of glyphosate, and the intermediate products are sarcosine and glycolic acid. In addition, Manassero et al. [71] studied the degradation process of glyphosate in the H2O2/UVC system and made more in-depth speculation on the mechanism of glyphosate degradation based on the aforementioned research results.

According to the results of this study and other past reports, it is reasonable to believe that the photocatalytic degradation of glyphosate involves a complex degradation mechanism. The possible degradation pathway of glyphosate in the GQDs/TNAs photocatalysis system was preliminarily speculated (Fig. 13 ). Under the action of ∙OH and ∙O2 –, the CN bonds were broken at steps ① and ② in the glyphosate molecule, respectively. The formation of glycolic acid and AMPA occurred in the case of bond ① breakage (pathway 1), while the formation of methyl phosphonic acid and glycine occurred in the case of bond ② cleavage (pathway 2). In pathway 1, glycolic acid was converted to oxalic acid and finally mineralized. Meanwhile, AMPA was transformed into NH4 + and methyl phosphonic acid through oxidative deamination, and methyl phosphonic acid (including the one produced by pathway 2) was further mineralized.

In pathway 2, glycine, a primary product, was oxidized and decomposed directly. Since the presence of acetic acid could not be confirmed in the degradation process, it was speculated that the CN bond broke to produce glycolic acid and NH4 + or the CC bond broke to produce CO2 and methylamine, which were eventually mineralized. A contradictory finding was the production of alanine and serine because these intermediates were not confirmed or speculated in similar past studies. The photocatalytic degradation process generally involves the category of free radical reaction. One of the possible ways is to form three carbon free radicals (the red circle in Fig. 13) in the photocatalytic degradation system of GQDs/TNAs and then react to produce these amino acids. Sirirote et al. [72] reported the biosynthesis of serine from glycine and methanol. However, whether similar processes can be achieved by chemical methods remains unclear. The production mechanism of amino acids in the degradation process needs to be explored in the future. In addition, this could not speculate whether CP bond breakage in glyphosate molecule for the production of sarcosine occurred in the degradation system, owing to the production of sarcosine cannot be confirmed through qualitative detection.

The stability of a photocatalyst is an important indicator to evaluate its practical application. To evaluate the stability of the GQDs/TNAs photocatalyst developed in this study, the degradation efficiency of the GQDs/TNAs photocatalyst towards the glyphosate was investigated by recycling the photoelectrodes for 15 cycles of 60 min each under UV–Vis light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 14 , the GQDs/TNAs photocatalyst exhibited excellent glyphosate degradation efficiency, reaching above 94% in each cycle. The results of the cycling experiments indicated that the GQDs/TNAs photocatalysts had an excellent cycling performance, which can be attributed to the strong combination of GQDs and TNAs. Based on the abovementioned results, we can conclude that the introduction of GQDs could promote the separation of photoelectron–hole pairs. The photoelectron–hole separation could efficiently and stably enhance the photodegradation of glyphosate, which endows GQDs/TNAs photocatalysts with practical application value.

In summary, the present work demonstrated a feasible strategy to prepare GQDs/TNAs heterojunction photocatalysts. The GQDs/TNAs heterojunction photocatalysts were found to degrade glyphosate with high efficiency (95%–99%) under 60-min UV–Vis light irradiation [9,10]. The prepared GQDs/TNAs composites showed better electrical conductivity and optical absorption [34,36,37] compared with pure TNAs photocatalysts, endowing composite photocatalysts with excellent photocatalytic activity for glyphosate. During the degradation of glyphosate, the increase in both the initial concentration and pH value of glyphosate reduced the degradation efficiency of glyphosate. The degradation reaction was influenced by some coexisting ions, and Cl– and SO4 2– showed an inhibitory effect on the degradation of glyphosate, while NO3 –, Fe3+, and Mn2+ had a positive effect. Moreover, the degradation mechanism of glyphosate is more complicated than previous reports, and further investigation is needed. In future work, GQDs/TNAs composites may be more developed and applied to the fields of photocatalytic degradation [36], hydrogen production [73], and solar cells [74] due to its excellent photoelectric properties.

Yajie Zhang: Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Hao Cao: Visualization, Investigation. Jinren Lu: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Methodology, Software. Yiming Li: Resources, Validation, Supervision. Mutai Bao: Resources, Supervision, Data curation.
